explanation: The natural language describes the fail-safe mode in which the controller forces the input valve to close and the output valve to open. The atomic proposition 'failSafeMode' captures when the system is in this mode. The atomic propositions 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' indicate the desired positions of the valves. To specify the required relationship, we use a global (G) property that says: globally, whenever the system is in fail-safe mode, it must be the case that the in valve is closed and the out valve is open. This guarantees that, at all times during fail-safe mode, the valve positions are enforced as described. No numerical or comparison operators are used, only Boolean logic and LTL temporal operators.

The natural language input describes the fail-safe requirement: when in fail-safe mode, the controller must force the input valve to close and the output valve to open. We express this as: globally, if failSafeMode holds, then inValveClose and outValveOpen must both hold simultaneously. This LTL property ensures that at every time the system is in fail-safe mode, the valves are forced to the required states. No numerical comparisons are needed, only references to atomic propositions representing failSafeMode, inValveClose, and outValveOpen. This directly and precisely captures the natural language requirement. Previous possible properties that do not directly tie the valve states to failSafeMode or use temporal operators incorrectly would fail to ensure the valves are always in the correct state during failSafeMode. This LTL guarantees the linkage at all times.

The natural language states that in fail-safe mode, the controller forces the input (inspiratory) valve to close and the output (expiratory) valve to open. The atomic propositions 'failSafeMode', 'inValveClose', and 'outValveOpen' are given, matching the fail-safe state and the valve status. The most direct and accurate LTL translation should require that whenever failSafeMode is active, it is always the case that 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' are true. Formally, this is captured as: G(failSafeMode -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)). This expresses that globally, whenever failSafeMode holds, both valves are guaranteed to be in the required de-energized (safe) positions. Previous LTL properties would be incomplete if they do not capture both the dependency on failSafeMode and the conjunction of both valve states whenever it is active. This formulation directly implements the described fail-safe requirement.

The input natural language describes the behavior in fail-safe mode: the controller forces the input (inspiratory) valve to close and the output (expiratory) valve to open. Based on the provided atomic propositions, 'failSafeMode' indicates the system is in fail-safe, 'inValveClose' describes that the inspiratory valve is closed, and 'outValveOpen' means the expiratory valve is open. The LTL formula uses the 'globally' (G) operator to ensure that whenever fail-safe mode is active, both the in-valve must be closed and the out-valve open, thus capturing the desired requirement at all points in time. This is the most direct and likely interpretation given the available propositions and the specification description.

The input specifies that in the fail-safe mode, the controller forces the input valve closed and the output valve open. The relevant atomic propositions are: 'failSafeMode' (indicating fail-safe mode is active), 'inValveClose' (input valve is closed), and 'outValveOpen' (output valve is open). The property should guarantee that globally, whenever failSafeMode is active, both inValveClose and outValveOpen hold. The LTL property thus uses the semantics G(failSafeMode -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)). This ensures that at all times, if the system is in failSafeMode, then both valves are in their required safe states; if not in failSafeMode, there is no imposed requirement. This most directly captures the English requirement; alternative LTLs that do not use implications or that do not require both valves simultaneously may not sufficiently enforce the fail-safe condition as described.

The natural language description specifies the fail-safe behavior: 'the controller forces input and output valves to their de-energized states (in valve close and out valve open).' According to the atomic propositions, failSafeMode represents the fail-safe condition, inValveClose represents that the inspiratory (input) valve is closed, and outValveOpen represents that the expiratory (output) valve is open. Therefore, the requirement is that whenever the controller is in failSafeMode, it should enforce inValveClose and outValveOpen. In LTL, this is captured as: globally, whenever failSafeMode is true, inValveClose and outValveOpen must both hold. Thus, the correct LTL formula is G(failSafeMode -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)). This ensures that at all times when fail-safe mode is entered, the valves are in their required state. Previous formulas that do not include the implication or fail to capture the necessity of both valve positions during failSafeMode would be incomplete or incorrect.

The input natural language describes the fail-safe requirement that, whenever the controller enters fail-safe mode, it forces the input (inspiratory) valve to be closed and the output (expiratory) valve to be open. In LTL, this can be captured as a safety property: 'whenever failSafeMode holds, inValveClose and outValveOpen also hold.' This is written as G(failSafeMode -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)). This ensures that at all times, if the system enters fail-safe mode, the required valve states are maintained. This property accurately models the described behavior; other possible LTL forms like guarantees after one step (using X) would not fully capture the requirement that the valve states must be maintained simultaneously with or for the duration of failSafeMode. Thus, the chosen property most faithfully represents the input specification.

The natural language describes a fail-safe scenario where the controller must ensure the 'input valve' is closed (inValveClose) and the 'output valve' is open (outValveOpen) whenever the fail-safe mode is active. The atomic proposition 'failSafeMode' indicates when fail-safe mode is active. To capture the requirement that during fail-safe mode, the in-valve is closed and the out-valve is open, we use the LTL formula: globally, whenever 'failSafeMode' holds, then 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must also hold. This is expressed as G(failSafeMode -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)). This property directly formalizes the requirement from the input natural language without resorting to comparisons or numeric thresholds, adhering to the required LTL grammar and atomic proposition usage.

The natural language specifies that, in fail-safe mode, the controller must force the input (inspiratory) valve to its closed state and the output (expiratory) valve to its open state. The atomic proposition 'failSafeMode' indicates when the controller enters fail-safe mode, 'inValveClose' indicates the inspiratory valve is closed, and 'outValveOpen' indicates the expiratory valve is open. The requirement can be captured as: whenever failSafeMode holds, it must always be the case that both inValveClose and outValveOpen are asserted simultaneously. Using LTL, this is expressed as 'G (failSafeMode -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen))', which globally enforces the valve states upon entering fail-safe mode. This is the most direct interpretation based on the available atomic propositions and the original specification.

The natural language input specifies that in fail-safe mode, the controller must force the input valve to 'close' and the output valve to 'open'. Among the available atomic propositions, 'failSafeMode' indicates that the system has entered fail-safe mode, 'inValveClose' means the inspiratory valve is closed, and 'outValveOpen' means the expiratory (output) valve is open. To express that whenever fail-safe mode holds, the in valve is closed and the out valve is open, we use the 'globally' operator G with the implication: if failSafeMode is active, then inValveClose AND outValveOpen both hold. This ensures the enforceable safe state throughout the duration that the system is in fail-safe mode, capturing the input as described. No numerical comparison operators are used, only atomic propositions and boolean/LTL operators as required.
